q1_speech.text: Can the patient speak in full sentences, or are they only able to say one or two words at a time?

q2_position.text: Is the patient able to find a comfortable position, such as sitting upright or leaning forward?

q3_stridor_wheeze.text: Can you hear any high-pitched sounds when the patient breathes in or out â€” stridor (a harsh, high-pitched sound when breathing in) or wheeze (a whistling sound when breathing out)?
q3_stridor_wheeze.guidance_note: Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction and is immediately life-threatening. Wheeze indicates lower airway narrowing (asthma, COPD) and may respond to bronchodilators if available.

q4_able_to_use_inhaler.text: Does the patient have their own inhaler (reliever) with them, and are they able to use it?

q5_spO2.text: Do you have a pulse oximeter available, and if so what is the oxygen saturation reading? If you do not have one, answer 'not available'.

pre_arrival_instructions: Stay on the line with me.
Help the patient sit upright in a position of comfort â€” most people breathe better sitting up.
Loosen any tight clothing around the chest or neck.
If oxygen is available, apply it now.
Monitor the patient closely. If they become more breathless, can only speak one or two words, or their lips turn blue, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
This is a serious breathing problem â€” help is on the way.
Help the patient sit upright in a position of comfort if possible.
If oxygen is available, apply it now.
Watch closely â€” if the patient becomes less responsive, stops moving, or turns blue around the lips, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
Help the patient use their inhaler â€” shake it well, have them breathe out fully, then spray the medicine while they breathe in slowly and deeply.
After spraying, ask them to hold their breath for 10 seconds if possible.
They can repeat the inhaler 3-4 times, one puff at a time, waiting 30 seconds between each puff.
If they have a spacer device, use that â€” it makes the medicine work better.
If the inhaler does not help after 3-4 puffs, or if they become worse, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
The oxygen level is dangerously low â€” help is on the way.
Help the patient sit upright.
If oxygen is available, apply it now at the highest flow.
Do not leave the patient alone.
If the patient becomes less responsive or stops moving, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
Help the patient sit upright in a position of comfort.
Loosen any tight clothing around the chest or neck.
If oxygen is available, apply it now.
Watch the patient closely. If they become more breathless, turn blue around the lips, or become less responsive, tell me immediately.
